text:
St Mary's Hospital 

Institute of Neurology 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Patel  
St Mary's Hospital  
Institute of Neurology, St Mary's Hospital, London W2 1NY  

Dear Dr Patel,  

Emily Carter, DOB: 17-08-1989, Hospital No: C672154 NHS No. 4728395612  
78 Willow Lane, London, W9 3HT 

Medication: 

Levetiracetam 1,000 mg twice daily. Lamotrigine 150 mg twice daily. Clobazam 10 mg at night as required for clusters.

Thank you for asking me to review this patient in our neurology clinic. She reports a notable deterioration in overall wellbeing over recent months following redundancy from her banking role. She describes heightened stress, irregular sleep patterns with delayed sleep onset and early waking, and intermittent missed doses during particularly disrupted nights. She has been using caffeine late in the day to combat fatigue. She denies alcohol binges but acknowledges occasional weekend drinking when socialising with former colleagues. There is no recent head injury, new medication, or intercurrent illness.

There has been little alteration in seizure frequency over the past year; she experiences one to two generalised tonic-clonic seizures weekly with the longest seizure-free period being three weeks. She has 3 absences per day. In addition, she suffers clusters of myoclonic jerks and occasional tonic seizures, these occur roughly once a month. No seizures have been recorded since her last appointment. While the objective pattern described above has remained broadly similar year-on-year, she perceives her condition as worsening because of increased post-ictal fatigue, longer recovery times, and greater interference with day-to-day activities, particularly since becoming unemployed. She associates bad nights of sleep and high-stress days with breakthrough events and more pronounced morning myoclonic clusters.

On discussion today, she wishes to avoid immediate medication changes if possible, hoping first to stabilise sleep and stress levels. I have reinforced the importance of strict adherence, morning and evening dosing windows, and avoiding abrupt omissions. She will keep a detailed seizure and sleep diary, including caffeine and alcohol intake, to help us quantify potential precipitants and the temporal relationship to events. She understands driving restrictions and safety advice regarding bathing, cooking, and heights.

Plans and recommendations (patient-reported goals agreed in clinic):
- Sleep hygiene: target regular bedtime/wake-time; minimise late caffeine; maintain a wind-down routine; consider smartphone reminders for doses.
- Stress management: she plans to engage with local employment support and has self-referred to talking therapies for anxiety related to job loss.
- Clobazam: to be used as previously advised for short myoclonic clusters, not more than two consecutive days without clinical review.
- Bloods today: U&E, LFT, FBC, vitamin D; to check general health and potential contributors to fatigue. No adverse effects from current therapy volunteered.

Given her perception of deterioration and the social impact, I will bring her back sooner than usual. If diaries confirm persistent clustering despite optimised adherence and sleep, we will discuss next steps in clinic, which may include rationalisation of current therapy or referral for further evaluation.

We will see her back in eight weeks, sooner if there is any escalation or concerns from you. 

Yours sincerely

She has 3 absences per day. In addition, she suffers clusters of myoclonic jerks and occasional tonic seizures, these occur roughly once a month. No seizures have been recorded since her last appointment

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: Identify explicit seizure frequencies mentioned: (1) "one to two generalised tonic-clonic seizures weekly" → 1 to 2 per week. (2) "She has 3 absences per day" → 3 per day. (3) "clusters of myoclonic jerks and occasional tonic seizures, these occur roughly once a month" → 1 cluster per month (no per-cluster count provided). Rule: choose the highest frequency. Comparing 3 per day vs 1 to 2 per week vs monthly clusters, the highest frequency is 3 per day. Check seizure-free status: Not seizure free (only up to three weeks seizure-free), and also "No seizures have been recorded since her last appointment" is too short and unspecified timeframe; thus not seizure-free for ≥6 months. Final label: 3 per day.
reference: There has been little alteration in seizure frequency over the past year; she experiences one to two generalised tonic-clonic seizures weekly with the longest seizure-free period being three weeks. She has 3 absences per day. In addition, she suffers clusters of myoclonic jerks and occasional tonic seizures, these occur roughly once a month. No seizures have been recorded since her last appointment
